title: Date Spine Generation
when_to_use: user needs to fill missing dates in a time series (e.g., zero-fill days with no orders)
building a daily/weekly/monthly calendar for joins before aggregation
creating a consistent time dimension to ensure no gaps appear in dashboards
preparing data for time-series forecasting where missing dates break models
enabling LEFT JOIN from a full date range to sparse event data
user asks 'how many events per day including days with zero?'
when_not_to_use: when data is already complete with no gaps — use as-is or the pattern adds unnecessary rows
when aggregating at coarser grain than the data frequency (e.g., weekly data has no weekly gaps) — use ts_date_trunc instead
when joining to precomputed calendar tables in a data warehouse — reuse existing dimensions
when only filtering by date range without needing intermediate rows — use date_trunc + filter instead
when working with very long date ranges (>10 years) on low-memory systems — generate_series may exceed memory; consider chunking or external spines
when building a continuous timeline where actual event timestamps must be preserved — use date_spine only for grain alignment

concept_explanation.what_it_does: A date spine is a synthetic table of consecutive dates (or time intervals) generated on the fly using generate_series, then joined with real data to ensure every time bucket appears in the output — even when no event occurred. It prevents gaps from appearing in dashboard visuals or analytics.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine printing a calendar for all days in 2024 and checking off which days have orders. A date spine creates that blank calendar; joining left ensures every date shows up — days without orders get NULL (or zero after COALESCE).
concept_explanation.key_behavior: generate_series(start, stop, step) must use compatible types — e.g., generate_series(DATE '2024-01-01', DATE '2024-12-31', INTERVAL '1 day') in DuckDB. The spine is independent of real data; it defines the grain, not the events.

template.minimal: SELECT generate_series('2024-01-01'::DATE, '2024-01-07'::DATE, INTERVAL '1 day') AS day
template.realistic:
-- Daily order count with zero-fill for missing days
WITH daily_orders AS (
  SELECT DATE_TRUNC('day', order_date) AS day, COUNT(*) AS orders
  FROM orders
  WHERE status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY 1
),
date_spine AS (
  SELECT generate_series(
    (SELECT MIN(day) FROM daily_orders),
    (SELECT MAX(day) FROM daily_orders),
    INTERVAL '1 day'
  )::DATE AS day
)
SELECT
  s.day,
  COALESCE(o.orders, 0) AS orders
FROM date_spine s
LEFT JOIN daily_orders o ON s.day = o.day
ORDER BY s.day
template.composable:
-- Revenue by day over last 30 days, including zero days (combines ts_date_spine + seg_case_when_classification)
-- Composes: ts_date_spine_generation + seg_case_when_classification
WITH daily_revenue AS (
  SELECT DATE_TRUNC('day', order_date) AS day, SUM(total) AS revenue
  FROM orders
  WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test')
  GROUP BY 1
),
date_spine AS (
  SELECT generate_series(
    (SELECT MAX(day) - INTERVAL '29 days' FROM daily_revenue),
    (SELECT MAX(day) FROM daily_revenue),
    INTERVAL '1 day'
  )::DATE AS day
),
spined_revenue AS (
  SELECT
    s.day,
    COALESCE(r.revenue, 0) AS revenue
  FROM date_spine s
  LEFT JOIN daily_revenue r ON s.day = r.day
)
SELECT
  day,
  revenue,
  CASE
    WHEN revenue = 0 THEN 'no_activity'
    WHEN revenue < 100 THEN 'low'
    WHEN revenue < 1000 THEN 'medium'
    ELSE 'high'
  END AS activity_tier
FROM spined_revenue
ORDER BY day

mistake_patterns.mistake: Missing CAST to DATE for generate_series output
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: generate_series(DATE, DATE, INTERVAL) returns a series of DATEs, but mixing types (e.g., DATE with TIMESTAMP) in JOIN conditions causes implicit casts and index non-use. In DuckDB, if the spine is DATE and data is TIMESTAMP, the JOIN fails or yields fewer rows than expected.
mistake_patterns.symptom: LEFT JOIN returns fewer rows than the spine — some dates are missing. Or query runs but filters out all rows with no matches.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT generate_series('2024-01-01', '2024-01-07', INTERVAL '1 day') AS day
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT generate_series('2024-01-01'::DATE, '2024-01-07'::DATE, INTERVAL '1 day')::DATE AS day

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using generate_series without filtering to relevant range
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Generating a full year of dates for a dataset spanning only 7 days wastes memory and CPU. DuckDB materializes the full series in memory before joining; for wide spines, this triggers OOM or excessive swap.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Query runs slowly or crashes with 'out of memory' — especially in interactive environments or with many concurrent users.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT generate_series('2020-01-01', '2024-12-31', INTERVAL '1 day') AS day
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT generate_series(
  (SELECT MIN(order_date) FROM orders),
  (SELECT MAX(order_date) FROM orders),
  INTERVAL '1 day'
) AS day

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using RIGHT JOIN instead of LEFT JOIN on spine
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: RIGHT JOIN starts from the real data table, which has gaps — the spine's purpose is violated. You end up with only days that exist in data, defeating zero-fill.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Result set has fewer rows than expected, and missing days reappear — spine is not applied.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT s.day, COALESCE(o.orders, 0) FROM date_spine s RIGHT JOIN daily_orders o ON s.day = o.day
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT s.day, COALESCE(o.orders, 0) FROM date_spine s LEFT JOIN daily_orders o ON s.day = o.day

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: generate_series(start, stop, step) with type-cast start/stop to DATE and INTERVAL step works natively. Supports DATE, TIMESTAMP, and numeric series.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same function name and syntax, but requires type-casted arguments (e.g., DATE '2024-01-01'). Very robust in DuckDB and Postgres.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Not supported. Uses GENERATE_DATE_ARRAY(start_date, end_date, INTERVAL day) or GENERATE_TIMESTAMP_ARRAY. Different argument order.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Use DuckDB's native syntax. For portability to BigQuery, generate spine in application layer or use a calendar table. Never rely on generate_series for cross-warehouse queries.

dialect_traps.construct: NULL handling in COALESCE with LEFT JOIN
dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: LEFT JOIN from date_spine yields NULL for missing days. COALESCE(orders, 0) works reliably. No NULL != 0 surprises.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Same syntax, but if data includes TIMESTAMP and spine is DATE, comparison may fail due to implicit cast mismatch; always align types.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Cast both sides to same type before JOIN: date_spine.day::TIMESTAMP = orders.order_date::DATE. This avoids silent mismatches.

choose_this_when: user explicitly asks for missing days/weeks/months filled with zero or last known value
dashboard needs time-series continuity for trend visualization
data has gaps due to weekends, holidays, or inactive periods
user questions 'how many per period including zero?'
building input for ML time-series models that require dense time grids

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants to aggregate by calendar period (e.g., monthly totals) and data is already sparse — gaps don't matter
choose_alternative_when.why: DATE_TRUNC on event timestamps is simpler than generating a full spine; spines are overkill when no gap-filling is needed

choose_alternative_when.condition: user needs a fixed calendar for fiscal years or non-standard weeks
choose_alternative_when.why: A lookup table of fiscal periods handles complex rules better than generate_series, which assumes uniform intervals

edge_cases.scenario: Data ends mid-day or contains partial periods
edge_cases.what_happens: Spine includes full days, but last day has incomplete events. Left join includes the day, but its metrics are understated.
edge_cases.handling: If only full periods matter, filter spine to end at LAST(date) WHERE day = (SELECT MAX(day) FROM daily_events) - INTERVAL '1 day'. Alternatively, aggregate to hour/minute grain if precision matters.

edge_cases.what_happens: generate_series on 3650+ rows may exceed DuckDB’s default memory per query or cause long planning times.
edge_cases.handling: Generate spine in chunks (e.g., yearly CTEs) or store a permanent calendar table. Alternatively, limit to ±1 year around MAX(date) if analysis window is recent.

edge_cases.scenario: Timezone-aware data with UTC vs local time
edge_cases.what_happens: Spine is generated in local time but events are stored in UTC. The JOIN misaligns days — events on Sunday evening UTC fall on Monday local time.
edge_cases.handling: Cast spine and data to same timezone: generate_series(..., INTERVAL '1 day') AT TIME ZONE 'America/New_York'. Use AT TIME ZONE consistently on both sides.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n) for spine generation, O(n log n) for the join sort
performance_notes.when_slow: Spines spanning >5 years on large datasets (e.g., daily spine for 10 years = 3650 rows; still small, but repeated for many users). Also slow when used in correlated subqueries.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Cache the spine as a permanent table (e.g., dim_dates) if the range is stable. For ad-hoc queries, restrict the spine to [MIN(date) - X, MAX(date)] via subqueries to avoid scanning irrelevant dates.
